text:
Financial performance (Q4 FY26 / FY26) — what changed and why it matters
Scale & growth: Global business ₹28.0 lakh crore (+12.11% YoY); deposits ₹15.68 lakh crore (+9.71%); advances ₹12.37 lakh crore (+15.30%).
Core spread metrics: Q4 NII ₹9,808 crore (+3.88% YoY); management stated NIM improved 9 bps QoQ and expects it to “hover around 2.5 to 2.6” (bank cited FY NIM ~2.51; Q4 NIM ~2.54 in analyst question).
Profitability (headline) vs underlying: FY operating profit ₹33,019 crore (+5.19%); FY net profit ₹19,187 crore (+12.69%).
Q4 profit was perceived weak QoQ; management attributed the QoQ drop in operating/net profit (~₹2,300 crore) largely to:
One-off listing gains in prior quarter: “listing gains… of ₹1,930 crore” from Canara HSBC and Canara Robeco stake dilution/listing-related income.
Treasury MTM losses: “bond yields moved from 6.59 to 7.05… share market corrected… [resulting] MTM losses of ₹800 crore.” Management expects this to be non-recurring near-term: “This may not be going ahead… may not be there in the June quarter… reversal will be seen… already it is showing in our books.”
Asset quality & cost of risk: GNPA 1.84% (down 110 bps YoY); NNPA 0.43% (down 27 bps); PCR 94.21% (+151 bps); credit cost 0.59% (down 33 bps); 12M slippage ratio 0.69% (down 21 bps).
Capital & liquidity: CRAR 17.04% (+71 bps); LCR 118% (vs 100% regulatory).
Shareholder payout: Proposed dividend 210% i.e., ₹4.20/share (FV ₹2). Management flagged that forward EPS/ROE guidance is conservative since “₹1,930 crore… one-time income… next year it won’t be there.”
Balance sheet mix & growth engine — clear tilt to RAM, with gold as a structural lever
Growth led by RAM: RAM book ₹7.30 lakh crore (+19.73%); retail ₹2.96 lakh crore (+32.93%); housing ₹1.24 lakh crore (+17.55%); vehicle ₹26,070 crore (+26.33%); MSME ₹1.57 lakh crore (+12.85%).
Target mix: Management aims to maintain ~“60-40” (RAM-corporate). On current status: “We endeavour to reach at 60-40. 59 RAM and 40 corporate.”
Gold loans now material (~20% of book):
Outstanding ₹2.45 lakh crore, of which Agri ₹1.54 lakh crore and non-agri ₹91,000 crore.
Management expects double-digit growth supported by branch footprint and regional customer behavior: “traditionally… branches… in South India… on asset side, we have this advantage of gold loans.”
Pricing unchanged: On interest rates, management explicitly said “No, we have not increased.”
Risk controls after fraud/newsflow: Enhanced controls include quarterly reappraisal (same quarter disbursals reappraised next quarter), dedicated gold loan officers at high-value branches, enhanced 24x7 security, TRTL safes, use of panel appraisers, and reappraisal by a different valuer to detect “spurious” collateral. Also: “And that is insured also.”
Operational lens on agri-vs-retail gold: management indicated portfolio is being managed in compliance with RBI guidelines, with slower agri growth offset by retail: “whatever loss you are seeing in Agri is compensated in retail… This is mostly in compliance with RBI guidelines we have devised this.” (They also said agri gold growth ~15% in one response.)
Asset quality trajectory — SMA improving despite growth; slippage composition provided
SMA trends (key comfort point for management): Total SMA 2.75% and falling in absolute terms despite loan growth:
SMA outstanding Dec’25: ₹35,604 crore → Mar’26: ₹33,728 crore
SMA Mar’25: ₹40,481 crore → Mar’26: ₹33,728 crore
SMA buckets (Mar’26): SMA-0 ₹10,961 crore; SMA-1 ₹13,035 crore; SMA-2 ₹9,732 crore.
Management repeatedly positioned SMA and slippage as “industry best” and used this to argue credit cost sustainability even under ECL.
Q4 slippages (₹2,771 crore) breakup (management disclosed):
MSME ₹1,333 crore
Agriculture ₹886 crore
Retail ₹431 crore
Corporate ₹80 crore
Gold loans ₹41 crore
Stress commentary / geopolitical: Management does not see incremental stress: comparing March-to-March, slippages were similar (~₹2,702 crore last March vs ~₹2,771 crore this March), and noted March quarter has audit-driven MOCs.
ECL (Expected Credit Loss) readiness — quantified transition, staging sensitivities, implementation plan
Management’s central message: Can absorb ECL impact comfortably; sees main incremental requirement in Stage 2.
Staging view vs IRAC:
“Stage 1 and stage 3… almost at par with the IRAC norms.”
Stage 2 is the key delta due to regulatory floor change: “regulatory floor has been moved from 0.4 to 5%… additional provision… presume… ₹2,500 crore.”
Aggregate impact estimate (bank-level, preliminary):
Stage 2 incremental: ~₹2,500 crore
PD-related add-ons across stages: ₹2,500–₹5,000 crore
Additional non-fund requirement: ₹2,500 crore
Total indicated range: ~₹10,000 crore (management’s working number), with ability to stagger over 4 years.
If fully absorbed upfront: “drop of 1% in the CRAR”; management stressed CRAR 17.04% provides buffer.
Execution plan / governance: Bank has engaged Ernst & Young as knowledge partner; system-level implementation targeted for September. Stage 2 provisioning number not shared yet: “yet to be implemented… Once system level implementation takes place then we will share this number.”
Run-rate credit cost under ECL: Management did not quantify but asserted sustainability: “I presume that we will maintain this [credit cost]” citing falling SMA and stable slippage expectations. Also reaffirmed ability to sustain ~1%+ ROA even through transition: “Sir, I presume yes. Bank can deliver that.”
Guidance, operating levers, and near-term outlook (FY27 posture)
Credit growth guidance: 11–12% (seen as conservative). Management rationale: GDP ~6.9% and internal tradition of conservatism; confidence to beat: “bank has a tradition of giving some conservative numbers… confident… will end up much above the guidance.”
NIM guidance: 2.5–2.6, with emphasis on:
avoiding low-yield assets: “We are not entertaining low yield advances.”
mix shift to RAM and pricing discipline
bulk deposit pricing focus: “very, very conscious on bulk deposits”
deposit repricing tactic: TD rate hikes were intended to substitute bulk with retail term deposits: “retail term deposit… much cheaper than the bulk deposit rate… idea… more flow in retail term deposit rather than bulk… that is what exactly has happened… benefited us.”
Deposit structure clarification: No floating-rate deposit product: “In deposit nothing is there which is floating in nature.” (followed by clarification on demand vs term deposits).
PSLC income: Management expects continuation around historical level:
“PSLC income is around ₹2,500 crore… continuously” and “we will gain advantage out of selling the PSLC.”
Later clarified expectation “around ₹3,000 [crore]” (there is some inconsistency in responses, but management’s intent is “similar/sustained”).
Recovery/TWO (technical write-off) expectations:
Typical TWO recovery cadence: ₹1,500–₹1,600 crore per quarter, FY total ₹6,500 crore (vs ₹6,800 crore prior year), implying stability rather than structural decline.
TWO interest income contribution: ₹350–₹400 crore per quarter.
Q1 early indicator: “one big ticket part amount has been recovered of ₹300 [crore]” and confidence on quarterly recovery run-rate.
ECLGS 5.0 (government scheme) impact (as discussed):
Bank identified affected portfolio ~₹90,000 crore; incremental exposure expected ₹18,000–₹20,000 crore, which “will give some flip to the advances.”
Corporate pipeline & undisbursed: Corporate sanctions pipeline ~₹20,000 crore and undisbursed corporate ~₹20,000 crore; management expects this plus ECLGS to support meeting/exceeding growth guidance.
Current account decline: Attributed to four large-ticket accounts moving, without naming; management claims underlying CA is otherwise healthy.
Subsidiary stake monetization — no further planned dilution
After earlier dilutions in asset management/JV insurance-related entities (as cited): “14.5% in Canara HSBC and 13% in Robeco… gained some ₹1,930 crore… Going ahead we don’t foresee any dilution.”
